ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Wandouhuang. A0CKP (legume-based sweet snack) is the closest defensible parent in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Wandouhuang or a sufficiently close Chinese pea cake analogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Wandouhuang is approximately 150 g (6 small pieces), as sold by the restaurant in a single portion.

nutrient_source.title: Yellow split peas, cooked, boiled, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16087); adapted for sweetened pea cake formulation with added sugar (~15 g sugar per 100 g finished product) based on standard Wandouhuang recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Wandouhuang exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy cooked yellow split peas (NDB 16087) were used as the primary base. Sugar content was adjusted upward to approximately 15 g per 100 g to reflect the lightly sweetened palace-style formulation. Fat remains negligible as no oil is used in the traditional recipe. Sodium is low as no salt is added per the product description.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of legume-based foods: split peas (yellow, cooked) GI = 32 ± 5; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Wandouhuang (Chinese pea cake). The GI of 32 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked yellow split peas (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The pea cake is made primarily from yellow split peas with added sugar; the high fibre and protein content of the legume base is expected to maintain a low GI despite added sugar. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 44.25) / 100 ≈ 14.1, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 29.5 × 1.5 = 44.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~18 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Wandouhuang is very low in sodium (~27 mg per 150 g serving) and contains no added salt per the product description, making it a suitable occasional dessert for individuals managing hypertension; however, portion size should be monitored due to moderate sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~14 per serving) despite low GI base; added sugar content (~15 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the legume base confers a low glycemic index (GI ≈ 32), the added sugar in Wandouhuang raises the glycemic load to approximately 14 per 150 g serving, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and account for this dessert within their total daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre from yellow split peas
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wandouhuang is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, while providing approximately 6 g of dietary fibre per 150 g serving from the legume base, which supports LDL cholesterol management and is generally favourable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wandouhuang (豌豆黄, Pea Cake) is a traditional Chinese imperial court dessert originating from Beijing, historically associated with the Qing Dynasty palace cuisine. It is made by simmering yellow split peas until completely soft, blending them into a smooth paste, sweetening lightly with sugar, and then setting the mixture in moulds before chilling to produce a firm yet delicate, sliceable confection. The texture is smooth, dense, and slightly gelatinous — similar to a firm bean jelly — with a mild, subtly sweet flavour and a characteristic pale yellow colour derived entirely from the natural pigment of the peas. The dessert contains no preservatives, no oil, and no dairy, making it naturally vegan and gluten-free. Nutritionally, Wandouhuang is a moderate-energy dessert (~148 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~5.2 g per 100 g) and notable dietary fibre (~4.1 g per 100 g) from the legume base, distinguishing it from most refined-flour confections. Its glycemic index is low (GI ≈ 32) due to the intact legume matrix, though added sugar raises the glycemic load to approximately 14 per standard 150 g serving. Clinically, it is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management in moderate portions, while individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for its carbohydrate content. In Singapore, it is served as a traditional Chinese dessert item and is available at specialty Chinese dessert restaurants.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of Chinese pea cake?
How many calories in pea cake?
What is 豌豆黄 made of?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Wandouhuang exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked yellow split peas with a sugar adjustment to reflect the lightly sweetened formulation. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (cooked yellow split peas, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16087, cooked yellow split peas) with sugar adjusted to ~15 g per 100 g to reflect the lightly sweetened palace-style formulation. No direct database entry for Wandouhuang was found in any primary regional source. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based legume confection with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA source for the legume base. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a vegan plant-based food; B12 is not present in plant foods. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin D source in the ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked split peas. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for cooked yellow split peas. 7. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from cooked yellow split peas (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Wandouhuang was found. 8. Serving size of 150 g (6 pieces) is based on the product description stating 6 pieces per portion; individual piece weight estimated at ~25 g each, consistent with typical Wandouhuang portion sizes. 9. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Desserts' as this is a confection/dessert item. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish.
